local_names.hindi: उबले हुए मुर्गी के अंडे की जर्दी

aliases: उबले हुए मुर्गी के अंडे की जर्दी

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical hard-boiled chicken egg yolk weighs approximately 17 g, representing the yolk separated from a single large boiled egg.

nutrient_source.title: Hard-boiled chicken egg, yolk — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for hard-boiled chicken egg yolk (F01060012). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01128 (Egg, yolk, raw, fresh) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; this substitution is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Hard-boiled egg yolk contains only 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index set to -1 (sentinel: not applicable / not measurable) consistent with numeric hygiene requirements; gi_category is not_applicable.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat (9.6 g/100 g) with low sodium (47 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low and does not raise blood pressure directly; however, saturated fat at 9.6 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold and may contribute to arterial stiffness and endothelial dysfunction — limit consumption to 3–4 yolks per week and avoid pairing with other high-saturated-fat foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.2 g/100 g) with high protein and fat slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.2 g/100 g, producing minimal glycaemic impact; however, the very high cholesterol content (1250 mg/100 g) warrants limiting intake to 3–4 yolks per week in diabetic patients with concurrent dyslipidaemia, with individual LDL monitoring recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high dietary cholesterol (1250 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (9.6 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg yolk is among the highest dietary sources of cholesterol at 1250 mg/100 g, and saturated fat at 9.6 g/100 g may raise LDL-C; individuals with established cardiovascular disease or hypercholesterolaemia should limit intake to no more than 2–3 yolks per week and consult a dietitian for personalised guidance.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (9.6 g/100 g) exceeding the 5 g/100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 9.6 g/100 g may promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6); limit to 2–3 yolks per week and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as fatty fish, turmeric, and leafy greens.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hard-boiled chicken egg yolk is the separated yolk portion of a chicken egg that has been cooked by boiling in the shell in unsalted water until fully set, then deshelled and the yolk isolated from the white. The yolk is dense, crumbly, and pale to deep yellow in colour, with a rich, creamy, slightly sulphurous flavour. It is consumed widely across global cuisines as a standalone snack, salad ingredient, devilled egg filling, or garnish. Nutritionally, the yolk is the most nutrient-dense part of the egg: it contains 16.1 g protein, 31.7 g fat (of which approximately 9.6 g is saturated fat), and only 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, yielding 347 kcal. It is exceptionally high in cholesterol at 1250 mg/100 g, making it one of the richest dietary cholesterol sources. It also provides meaningful amounts of calcium (115 mg), iron (4.8 mg), and potassium (97 mg). The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, the yolk is suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns but requires moderation in individuals with cardiovascular disease, hypercholesterolaemia, or dyslipidaemia associated with type 2 diabetes. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a hard-boiled egg yolk?
Is egg yolk high in cholesterol?
Is egg yolk good for keto diet?
How much protein in egg yolk?
Is egg yolk bad for heart disease?
Hard boiled egg yolk fat content

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F01060012), an approved primary regional source, with exact match to the described food. Saturated, monounsaturated, and polyunsaturated fat fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01128 (egg yolk, raw, fresh) as the closest approved secondary source, with the adaptation documented.
notes: Saturated fat (9.6 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (12.1 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (4.2 g/100 g) values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01128 (Egg, yolk, raw, fresh) as HPB Foodo does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this entry; the overall fat total (31.7 g/100 g) is consistent with the HPB primary value. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 g — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin and mineral micronutrients beyond those reported by HPB (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient breakdown is not available in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific hard-boiled preparation; USDA FoodData Central NDB 01128 provides estimates for raw yolk but was not substituted here to avoid mixing preparation states for micronutrients. Glycemic index is not applicable — hard-boiled egg yolk contains negligible carbohydrates (0.2 g/100 g); glycemic_index is set to -1 (numeric sentinel indicating not applicable / not measurable for this food type) and gi_category to not_applicable, consistent with the critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods and the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g — confirmed zero per HPB source; egg yolk contains no dietary fibre by composition. Carbohydrate_g is 0.2 g — not zero; no zero-macro justification required for carbohydrate. Protein_g (16.1 g) and fat_g (31.7 g) are non-zero; no zero-macro justification required.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Hard-Boiled Chicken Egg, Yolk' is precise and unambiguous. Local-language names provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 for a universally consumed food with Singapore HPB provenance.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (347.0), protein_g (16.1), fat_g (31.7), and carbohydrate_g (0.2) all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo F01060012 (approved primary source). nutrient_source fully populated.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (47.0), saturated_fat_g (9.6), and sugar_g (0.2) all present as numeric values. Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01128 and documented in notes.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to -1 (numeric sentinel: not applicable / not measurable) — satisfies the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric while correctly representing that GI is not applicable for a food with negligible carbohydrates (0.2 g/100 g). gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source fully populated with explanation. Previous iterations failed because null is not numeric; -1 is the correct numeric sentinel for unavailable/not-applicable data per G3 numeric hygiene rules.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 (chicken egg yolk, food) — exact match. FoodEx2 code A00LC (Hen egg yolk) — exact match. SNOMED CT 102263004 (Egg yolk, substance) — exact match. All three anchors defensible and consistent.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, nutritional profile, clinical relevance, and dietary flags. common_user_queries and meal_context populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate concern, saturated fat key factor), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, negligible carbohydrate key factor), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern, cholesterol and saturated fat key factors). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 11 entries covering English variants, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. aliases_by_language populated for all relevant languages. Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language values verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — confirmed consistent.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to null and gi_category from null to 'not_applicable' to comply with the q4 critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

Iteration 3: Corrected glycemic_index from null to -1 (numeric sentinel) to satisfy the validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. null failed q4 because the scoring model requires a numeric value. -1 is the approved numeric sentinel for not-applicable / data-unavailable fields per G3 numeric hygiene rules, and is consistent with the template's use of -1 for fields where data is not available or not applicable.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. The q4 failure from iteration 2 (glycemic_index was null, which is non-numeric) has been resolved by setting glycemic_index to -1, the approved numeric sentinel for not-applicable data. gi_category remains not_applicable and gi_source documents the rationale. Core macros are complete and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (approved primary source). Fatty acid fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central with documented adaptation. Ontology anchors are defensible across FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. Multilingual names provided in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. All three required health conditions present with clinical specificity. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent between flat array and aliases_by_language.
